subject: science
chapter_title: Exploration: Entering the World of Secondary Science

primary.justification: The chapter's central instructional strand develops the concept of scientific models as deliberate simplifications that retain only the details relevant to the question at hand. It introduces the assumptions behind model-building (Example 1.1, Activity 1.1), the use of mathematical relationships to express and test models, and the role of predictions as reasoned expectations derived from laws and theories — directly mapping to C-8.1's requirement to develop models to represent real-life events and use them to predict results.

A distinct strand of the chapter addresses the design and evaluation of scientific inquiry: what makes a prediction scientifically testable (Example 1.2), the role of measurable evidence and past patterns in forming hypotheses, the skill of estimation to check whether answers are reasonable (Example 1.3), and the distinction between laws, theories, and principles as products of systematic inquiry. These map directly to C-8.2's formulation of hypotheses, identification of variables, and communication of findings using scientific terminology.

dissolution_test: This chapter builds the student's ability to recognise how models and predictions function in scientific inquiry by distinguishing which details to include or ignore when building a model and by evaluating whether predictions are grounded in measurable, pattern-based evidence rather than vague observation or opinion.
